steps.m: Three special letters: Alif, Lam, Ra
steps.n: These are individual letters that appear at the start of some surahs. They are pronounced by naming each letter separately: 'Alif, Lam, Ra'

steps.m: Alif Lam Ra (special letters whose full meaning is known to Allah)
steps.n: The letters are written connected with a special mark (مد) over the Alif. These letters appear at the start of Surah Ibrahim, drawing attention to the miraculous nature of the Quran.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changes from verb 'wrote' to noun meaning 'something written'

steps.m: a book (that is أَنزَلْنٰهُ/We sent down)
steps.n: The tanween (double damma) shows this is indefinite. This book is the subject of أَنزَلْنٰهُ (We sent it down) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding أ at the start changes the meaning to 'made something come down' instead of just 'came down' - like the difference between 'drop' and 'make drop'
The نا shows that 'we' did the action. This is a complete verb form and doesn't have a case ending.

steps.m: we sent it down (referring to كِتٰبٌ - the Book)
steps.n: The هُ refers back to كِتٰبٌ (the Book) mentioned earlier in the ayah
This is the basic preposition that shows direction or movement towards something
Changed to إِلَيْ with a sukun (little circle) to connect with pronouns that come after it

steps.m: to you (object of أَنزَلْنٰهُ - we sent it down)
steps.n: Added the pronoun كَ which means 'you'. This word shows who receives the action of sending down (أَنزَلْنٰهُ) in this ayah.

steps.m: to exit or make something exit

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added alif at beginning to make the verb causative - changing from 'exit' to 'make exit'
Changed to present tense 'you' form by adding تُ at start and changing middle vowel to kasra

steps.m: so that you make (something) exit (to bring out الناس/the people)
steps.n: Added لِ to show purpose - this verb explains why Allah sent down the book mentioned earlier in the ayah

steps.m: people (those who are social/familiar with each other)
steps.n: Changed from the root meaning 'to be social' to mean 'people' (humans who are social with each other). The أ was dropped to make it a noun.
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning, making it refer more strongly to all people
Added ال to specify these particular people being talked about in this verse

steps.m: the people (object of لِتُخْرِجَ - so you may bring out)
steps.n: Added َ to show this word is receiving the action of bringing out mentioned in لِتُخْرِجَ (so you may bring out)
This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from
Added َ (fatha) at the end because it connects to the next word الظُّلُمٰتِ (darkness). This helps make the words flow together smoothly when reading.
Root letters got short 'u' vowels to make the plural form meaning 'darknesses'
Changes the regular plural to a feminine plural by adding ات at the end
Adding ال makes it definite. This word comes after مِنَ (from) in the ayah.
Adding shadda (ّ) doubles the ل to emphasize the darkness
The kasra ending shows this word follows مِنَ (from) in the ayah
This is a preposition that shows direction. In this ayah, it connects the verb لِتُخْرِجَ (to bring out) with its destination ٱلنُّورِ (the light), showing where the people are being brought to.
Connected the root letters together to make the basic word for light
The damma makes this word refer to divine or spiritual light rather than ordinary light
Doubling the middle letter makes the light more intense/powerful
Added ال to show this is the specific divine light mentioned in the Quran that guides people from darkness

steps.m: to the intense divine light (connected to إِلَى)
steps.n: The kasra at the end shows this word comes after إِلَى (to/towards) in the ayah
Changed the action of allowing into the noun 'permission' that we can use in a sentence
Added بِ at the beginning to mean 'by' and ِ at the end to show this connects to the next word (their Lord)
The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this a noun for someone who does this action completely
Adding kasra at the end shows this connects to what comes after it
Adding هِمْ at the end shows this belongs to them
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination
Added an elongation mark (ٰ) which extends the sound but doesn't change the meaning. This word connects to النُّورِ (the light) in this ayah, showing the direction or destination.
The pattern فِعَال is used for nouns meaning paths or ways
Changed the alif to an alif khanjariya (dagger alif) - this is just a writing variation and doesn't change the meaning
Added kasra at the end to show this word belongs to العَزِيزِ (The Mighty) which comes right after it
Added ي between the last two letters and vowels to show this describes someone who has this quality very strongly
Added ال to make it specific and ِ at the end to show it's being described or owned by something else in the sentence
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added ي to show this is a quality someone has
Added ال (the) to make it mean 'The Praiseworthy One' as a name of Allah

steps.m: The Praiseworthy One (possessed by/connected to العَزِيزِ)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this is possessed by or connected to العَزِيزِ (The Mighty) in the phrase صِرٰطِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَمِيدِ (path of the Mighty, the Praiseworthy)
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding vowel patterns

This is the unique name for the one true God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the'), the shadda emphasizes it, and the kasra at the end shows it's connected to what comes after it in the sentence.
Base form of the relative pronoun that connects parts of a sentence, like 'who' or 'which' in English

steps.m: the one who/which (owns مَا فِى ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ - what is in the heavens)
steps.n: Added ال with shadda to make it definite and specific. In this ayah, it refers to Allah who owns what is in the heavens.
Added fatha to show the vowel sound
Added the pronoun 'him/it' to show what belongs to Allah
Added a small و mark to help with pronunciation
This is a relative pronoun that refers to everything in the heavens. In this context, it's used to express Allah's ownership over all things.
This is a preposition that shows location or position. In this ayah, it connects 'what' (ما) with 'the heavens' (السَّمٰوٰتِ) to say 'what is in the heavens'.
Adds اء ending to make it a noun referring to something high above
Changes sky/heaven to its plural form by adding -waat at the end
Adding the definite article makes the noun definite

steps.m: the heavens/skies (possessed by/belonging to what is mentioned after)
steps.n: The word now shows it belongs to or is owned by what comes after it (shown by the -i ending)
Starting with the basic word 'what' which is used to ask questions or refer to things in general
Added و at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it. In this ayah, it's connecting to list what belongs to Allah - both what's in the heavens AND what's in the earth.
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment. Here it connects 'what' (ما) with 'the heavens' (السَّمٰوٰتِ) to mean 'what is in the heavens'
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make them pronounceable
Adding ٱلْ (al) makes it refer to the specific earth we know

steps.m: of the earth/ground/land (after فِى in the ayah)
steps.n: The kasra at the end shows this word follows the word فِى (in/within) in this ayah
This is a word used to express severe warning or threat
Adds a mark (ٌ) at the end to show this word is doing the action
Adds conjunction 'and' at beginning

steps.m: to reject or cover up truth
steps.n: Basic past tense form showing someone did this action
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to describe someone who does this action
Makes it plural to refer to multiple people

steps.m: the ones who reject/cover up truth
steps.n: Makes it specific by adding 'the'

steps.m: for the ones who reject/cover up truth
steps.n: Adds 'for/to' meaning

steps.m: for the ones who reject/cover up truth (connected to وَيْلٌ)
steps.n: Added emphasis with shadda on ل and final vowel to show connection to وَيْلٌ (woe)
This is a basic preposition showing that the 'woe' comes from/because of the punishment. It connects the word عَذَابٍ (punishment) to وَيْلٌ (woe).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
The verb 'to punish/cause pain' becomes a noun meaning 'punishment'. Like changing 'hit' to 'a hit'. Here it's connected to the word 'severe' to mean 'severe punishment'.
The tanween kasra (ٍ) shows this is indefinite (like 'a/an' in English) and is possessed by the word شَدِيدٍ (severe), forming the phrase 'a severe punishment'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changes the verb into an adjective describing how intense/strong something is. Here it's describing the punishment (عَذَابٍ).
The ٍ (kasratan) shows this word belongs to or is connected to something else
This is a special word used to point to people or things we're talking about
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
The 'است' prefix changes 'love' into 'seeking/preferring something over another thing'
Changed to present tense with ي prefix, and doubled the middle letter with vowel changes

steps.m: they prefer/choose (over something else) (subject of ٱلَّذِينَ)
steps.n: Added plural ending to show multiple people doing the action. This word describes what 'those who' (ٱلَّذِينَ) are doing.
Added و between second and third root letters to create a noun meaning 'life'
Added ة at the end to make it a feminine noun, and elongated the vowel with ٰ
Added ٱلْ at the beginning to make it definite (like 'the' in English)
Added َ (fatha) at the end to show this word is the object of the verb يَسْتَحِبُّونَ (they love/prefer)
Changed the root letters to make a special word for 'this world' - called this because it's the life closest to us right now
Added 'the' at the beginning which made the د double in strength. This word describes 'life' (الْحَيَوٰةَ) in the verse, telling us which life is being talked about.
This is a basic word that shows one thing is over or preferred above another. In this ayah, it shows the worldly life is being chosen over the hereafter.

steps.m: to come after, to be delayed

steps.n: Added long 'aa' sound after first letter and 'i' sound in middle to show 'one who comes after'

steps.m: the life that comes after (this one)
steps.n: Added ة to change from 'last' to mean specifically 'the afterlife' or 'next life'
Added ال to show we're talking about THE afterlife, specifically contrasting with this worldly life mentioned in the ayah
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word عَلَى (upon/over) in the ayah
Connected the root letters and doubled the middle letter to show strong prevention
Added يَ at start and changed middle vowel to ُ to show ongoing action
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
Added و at start to connect this action with what came before. In this ayah, they are preventing others from Allah's path.
This is a basic preposition that shows movement away from something or separation. In this ayah, it's used with سَبِيلِ (path) to show people turning away from Allah's path.
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to make a path or way

steps.n: Adding ي and vowels creates the pattern for 'path'
Added kasra at the end because it's connected to 'Allah' - like saying 'path of Allah'
Added ال to specify this is THE one true God, and the hamza was dropped because this is a special word used so often. The middle letter ل gets a shadda to show emphasis.
Added kasra at the end to show this is possessed by the word سَبِيلِ (path), making it mean 'path of Allah'. Also, the first alif changed to a waṣla (ٱ) because it connects with the previous word.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the past tense verb
Added و (and) to connect this action with what came before
Changed to present tense by adding ي at start

steps.m: and they seek wrongfully (referring to those who prefer worldly life)
steps.n: Makes the verb plural masculine to match the subject 'those who prefer' (ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَحِبُّونَ) from earlier in the ayah

steps.m: and they seek it wrongfully (referring to crookedness in Allah's path)
steps.n: Adds feminine object pronoun referring to عِوَجًا (crookedness) which comes right after this word in the ayah

steps.m: to be crooked or deviate from straightness

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to be crooked' to noun meaning 'crookedness'

steps.m: crookedness (as the object being sought in يَبْغُونَهَا - they seek it)
steps.n: Added ًا to show this is what's being sought in the phrase 'they seek it crooked'. This connects to يَبْغُونَهَا (they seek it) in the ayah.
This is the base form of the demonstrative pronoun used to point to things that are far away
Added hamza to show this refers to multiple things/people
Added ك at the end to show we are pointing to specific things/people

steps.m: those (referring to the disbelievers mentioned before who prefer worldly life)
steps.n: Added vowel marks and extended alif to complete the word. This word refers back to the disbelievers described in the previous part of the verse.

steps.m: in (shows they are in ضَلٰلٍۭ - misguidance)
steps.n: Basic preposition showing something is inside or within something else. Here it shows the disbelievers are deeply in misguidance.

steps.m: in (shows they are in ضَلٰلٍۭ - misguidance)
steps.n: Added kasra (ِ) mark under ف to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'fee'

steps.m: in (shows they are in ضَلٰلٍۭ - misguidance)
steps.n: Changed the final ي to ى which is just a writing style variation - it means the same thing

steps.m: to wander off the right path

steps.n: Added vowel marks (fatha) to the root letters to make it mean 'going astray' or 'wandering off the path'
Added alif after second letter to make it into an abstract concept
Added tanween kasra to show it's indefinite and in genitive case

steps.m: a complete state of being lost (in this ayah: describing how far they have strayed)
steps.n: The alif (ا) is written as a small vertical line above the second letter to show a long 'aa' sound, with a tanwin kasra (two slanted lines) at the end to show it's indefinite
Added vowels and ي to make an adjective describing something that is distant
Added tanwin kasra to show this word describes the type of misguidance (ضَلٰلٍۭ) mentioned in the ayah

steps.m: not/what (introduces a negative statement or question)
steps.n: This is a basic particle that can mean either 'not' or 'what' depending on context. In this ayah, it works with أَرْسَلْنَا (we sent) to mean 'we did not send'

steps.m: not/what (introduces a negative statement or question)
steps.n: The alif is lengthened to show proper pronunciation, but this doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: and not/and what (connects to previous statement while introducing a negative)
steps.n: Adding و at the beginning connects this idea to what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start and sukun on first root letter makes the verb causative - showing someone made the action happen
Adding نَا shows 'we' did the action. The sukun before نَا is needed for pronunciation. This verb connects to وَمَآ (and not) at the start of the ayah.

steps.m: from (connecting رَّسُولٍ to what comes before)
steps.n: This is a preposition that connects رَّسُولٍ (messenger) to the previous phrase, showing where the messenger comes from
The و makes it mean someone who does this action as their main role
Adds tanween kasra to show this is indefinite and in genitive case
This is a basic particle that shows an exception. In this ayah, it connects to رَّسُولٍ (messenger) to show that Allah never sent any messenger EXCEPT with a specific condition.
Added vowels and alif to make the noun for the thing that does the expressing (tongue/language)

steps.m: with/in the language (of قَوْمِهِۦ - his people)
steps.n: Added بِ at beginning to show this is the means/instrument used, and ِ at end because it connects to 'his people' coming after it

steps.m: to stand together, rise as one

steps.n: The root meaning of standing together becomes a noun for a group that stands united as one people
The kasra (ِ) shows this word belongs to or is connected to something else
Adding هِۦ means 'his'. This word is connected to لِسَانِ (language), forming the phrase 'language of his people'
Added shadda to show intensive meaning of explaining thoroughly
Added يُ prefix to show present tense

steps.m: so that he makes clear (to قَوْمِهِۦ - his people)
steps.n: Added لِ to show purpose - this explains why the messenger was sent with the language of his people
Added fatha to show how the letter is pronounced
Added هُ to show who the preposition is directed to (him/it)
Added م with sukun to change 'him' to 'them'. This word connects to لِيُبَيِّنَ (to explain/make clear) in the ayah, showing who the explanation is for.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: he led astray, caused to go astray
steps.n: Doubling the ل changes it from 'being lost' to 'causing others to be lost'
Changed to present tense and causative form - now means making someone else go astray

steps.m: and so he leads astray (subject: اللَّهُ)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this happens as a result of what came before. This word is connected to اللَّهُ (Allah) who is doing the action.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding vowel patterns
This is the unique name for the one true God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the'), the shadda emphasizes the ل, and the ُ shows this is the subject of يُضِلُّ (leads astray) in this ayah.
This is a relative pronoun used for people. In this ayah, it refers to the person who Allah guides, showing who receives the action of guidance.

steps.m: to want or wish for something

steps.n: Adding the fatha (َ) at the end makes it a complete past tense verb
Added يَ at the beginning to show present tense and ُ at the end to show this is the doer. This verb connects to يَهْدِى (he guides) in the ayah, showing who is doing the guiding.

steps.m: to guide or show the right way

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense verb
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense, with sukun on first root letter and kasra on second root letter
Added وَ (and) to connect this word with the previous verb فَيُضِلُّ (so He misguides) in the ayah
This is a special word that refers to people in a general way. In this ayah, it shows who Allah leads astray - it could be any person. It's the object (receiver) of the verb يُضِلُّ (leads astray).
Added vowels and combined letters. The middle letter ي changes to ا because it's a weak letter.
Added يَ at the beginning to show it's happening now, and changed the ending to ُ to match
Added long ا to show the extended nature of wanting/wishing
Added vowel marks to show the correct pronunciation of the pronoun. This word is the subject of the next word العَزِيزُ (The Mighty) in this ayah.
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it.
Added short vowels (fat-ha and kasra) to make it mean 'he is mighty' instead of just the root meaning
Adding ي makes the quality stronger, like saying 'very' or 'extremely'
Adding ال and ُ makes this one of Allah's names, showing His perfect might and power
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Forms an intensive adjective showing someone filled with the quality of wisdom
Adding ال makes it definite, and the ُ shows it's describing Allah. This is one of Allah's names describing His perfect wisdom.
This is the base particle that shows certainty or completion of an action
Added ل at the beginning to add extra emphasis
Added و at the beginning to connect this statement with what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start makes it mean 'to cause someone to do the action'. The middle letter gets a sukun (ْ).
Adding نَا at the end shows 'we' did the action. This word is the main verb in this part of the ayah.

steps.m: Moses (the prophet sent to free the Children of Israel)
steps.n: This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew (מֹשֶׁה/Moshe) or Egyptian (ms/mesu meaning 'child/son'), not derived from an Arabic root. In this ayah, Moses is the object of أَرْسَلْنَا (We sent).

steps.m: to show a sign or miracle
steps.n: This root relates to showing or displaying something meaningful
Forms the singular noun for 'sign' by extending the first letter into آ and adding ة at the end
Makes the word plural by removing ة and adding ات
Adds نَا at the end to show these are Allah's signs being referred to
Adds بِ at the beginning to show these signs were used as instruments or means
This is a particle that connects two ideas - here it connects 'We sent' (أَرْسَلْنَا) with what Moses was sent to do. It works like 'to' in English phrases like 'told him to go'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Adding أَ at the beginning makes it causative - changing 'exit' to 'make exit'
Changed vowels to make it a command form. This is Allah commanding Moses to bring his people out of darkness.
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to form the noun meaning 'people' or 'folk'
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of the verb أَخْرِجْ (bring out)
Added the suffix ك to show possession - 'your people'. This entire word is what is being acted upon by the command verb أَخْرِجْ (bring out)
Basic preposition indicating starting point, source, or origin
Added fatha at the end because it connects to the next word الظُّلُمٰتِ (the darknesses). This connection shows we're talking about coming from specific darknesses.
Forms the plural noun 'darknesses'
Changed the root word into plural by adding 'u' sounds (like saying 'zulm' → 'zulum')
Added the feminine plural ending to show many different types of darkness

steps.m: the many different kinds of darkness
steps.n: Added 'the' at the beginning and doubled the first letter

steps.m: from the many different kinds of darkness (object of مِنَ)
steps.n: Added the kasra ending because it comes after مِنَ (from). This word is what is being come out from in the phrase 'bring out from the darknesses'.
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows movement or direction towards something
Added ى at the end which is standard for this preposition. In this ayah, it connects to the word النُّورِ (the light), showing movement from darkness to light.
Connected the root letters to form the basic noun for light
Adding the damma (ُ) vowel mark makes this a proper noun meaning light
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ن with shadda because it comes after ال
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word إِلَى (to/towards) in the ayah
The intensive form means 'to remind strongly'
Added fatha, shadda, and kasra to make it a strong command form
Added sukun at the end to complete the command form
Added هُم at the end to show who is being reminded
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this command with what came before it
Forms the plural noun 'days'
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun meaning 'day'
Adding أ at the start is the first step to make this word plural
Adding ا in the middle completes the plural pattern for this word
Changed to 'days of' by adding kasra at end and changing the last letter shape - this shows it belongs to Allah
Added preposition ب to show 'with/by'
Changed to the unique name for the One True God by adding ال and merging letters
Added ِ to show this belongs to 'the days of' (أَيَّىٰمِ). Also added shadda for emphasis and changed alif to wasl-alif since it comes after another word.
Adding the shadda (double letter mark) changes this from a basic word meaning 'if/that' into a stronger word that emphasizes what comes after it, like saying 'truly' or 'certainly' in English.
This is a preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. Here it connects أَيَّىٰمِ (days) to what comes before it.
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show it's pointing to something far away instead of nearby
Added لِ to strengthen the meaning of distance, like saying 'over there' instead of just 'there'
Added كَ at the end to complete the word. This makes it clearer that we're pointing to something specific

steps.m: to show, make clear, or demonstrate
steps.n: This root carries the basic meaning of making something clear or evident
Root letters combine with long 'aa' sound and special ending to make the noun meaning 'sign'
Changes the singular 'sign' into plural form
Adds emphasis to show these are definitely signs

steps.m: surely signs (connected to صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ - patient and grateful)
steps.n: Makes the word indefinite and shows it's connected to the description that follows (patient and grateful)
Forms the noun meaning 'all/every'
Added damma and doubled the middle letter to create the word meaning 'all' or 'every'
Added لِ at beginning to mean 'for' and kasra at end because it follows a preposition
Added shadda on the ل to emphasize the meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Added shadda and alif to show someone who is intensely or frequently patient

steps.m: a very patient one (after preposition لِ)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra to show this describes someone after the preposition لِ in لِكُلِّ (for every)
This root represents the basic concept of thanking or showing gratitude
Changed to a special pattern (فَعُل) that means someone who does a lot of thanking
Added و to make it an intensive form showing someone who is constantly and naturally very grateful
Added tanwin (double kasra) to show this describes an unspecified grateful person
This is a time particle that shows when something happened in the past. Here it introduces when Moses spoke to his people.

steps.m: and when (connecting to قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: The و connects this phrase to what came before it in the Quran, adding the meaning of 'and'
Added fatha vowels to make it past tense. This is the basic form before the weak letter change.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs

steps.m: Moses (the one speaking to قَوْمِهِ/his people)
steps.n: This is a proper name borrowed from ancient Egyptian meaning 'drawn out/saved from water'. It doesn't have an Arabic root. In this ayah, Moses is speaking to his people about remembering Allah's favors.

steps.m: to stand together as a group

steps.n: Root 'stand' became a noun meaning 'people' by adding vowels - like how a group stands together
Adds the preposition ل to indicate 'to/for'

steps.m: to/for his people (acted on by قَالَ)
steps.n: Adds هِ (meaning 'his') to show these are Moses's people who he is speaking to
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he remembered' to 'remember!' by adding اُ at the start - this makes it a command to one person
Changed to plural command by adding وا at end and changed initial alif to waṣla form
Changed from root letters to a simple noun meaning 'blessing' by adding the vowels kasra and sukoon
Added ة to show this is one specific instance of blessing
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of the command 'remember' (اذْكُرُوا)
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
Added ال and merged letters to create the proper name for the one true God
Added ٱ to help pronunciation when starting the word
Added kasra at the end to show this word belongs to or describes نِعْمَةَ (favor/blessing)
This is the basic preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'
The ى changes to يْ when adding a suffix - this is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning
Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being spoken to
This is a word that shows when something happened in the past. Here it introduces when Moses spoke to his people.
